What's up everybody? Welcome back. I probably just scared some of you guys were eating lunch and also started yelling at you, but I'm excited. So I want to start at the very beginning because this is something I didn't understand for a long time, but the way somebody presents you before you come on stage is huge. If you've read persuasion or understand pre-framing or anything, like this has a lot to do with it. I used to just let the promoter promote introducing however they wanted and what I found a lot of times the promoter would get there and be like, that's a little card that I up next is Russell Brunson. Russell likes to whatever. Let's put our hands together for Russell Brunson and they'll walk off and you're like, oh, and takes like 20 minutes to get the energy high enough to get him excited. So what I've done in the past, I have an intro video that introduces me and if somebody, if I don't think somebody's going to be good, introduce me, then I have them introduce the video I'm making play the video and the video introduces me, then I come through from the have the video introduced me because you have to control that pre-frame. You will see Armand Moran actually told me this initially. He talked about like, depending on how he introduces someone who has a huge effect on how they actually sell afterwards. And so that's a big thing. With this one, they wanted to introduce me. They want to do the the way that they did it. Overall, it was really good. One thing I asked them that they didn't do is they said, everyone let's give a warm welcome to Russell Brunson. You know, it's like some people stood up some day and it was kind of awkward. Like for me, I always want, when I'm pretty interested in anybody or if something interesting, I want people out of their chair. Someone's like, stand up and put your hands together for Russell Brunson. I told them specifically to say stand up. They didn't, but that just helps a lot because you get the right energy level. It's easier because it's hard to explain until you've been in a room. But like when you, like if I spoke, I spoke like a Dan Kennedy event versus like Tony Robbins event. It's so different. Dan Kennedy event, you walk in and everyone's like half asleep in the back of the room and you have to like take you 20 minutes to get everyone excited enough. You can actually serve them. Whereas Tony Robbins, like they're going nuts. You walk right out into that energy level and it's way easier. And so the intro is what sets that up for you. So make sure you control your intro, either through your own video or coach the person throughout to do it or whatever it is. Otherwise, it's a painful process to get the energy level up. Luckily, this room is insanely big and everything was really good. So it wasn't hard, but that's just something to kind of really consider that preframe of how you enter has a huge thing to do with how people pay attention and what they buy, what they don't buy and whole much stuff on top of that. If you watch YouTube videos, he'll get like Mass Group, Hypnosis. We can help as much people at the train station, do something, I'll pass out and fall over and stuff like that. And what he did, a whole documentary, and it's called Push. And the premise of the documentary was, can we get somebody in a finite period of time, I can't remember if it's 60 minutes or 90 minutes, something like that, can we get them using social cues, social pressure and things like that to actually commit murder? So that's the premise of this documentary. And then, and they set the whole thing up and they walked through how they did it. And they have four people go through this experiment and three of the four people actually commit murder at the end of it, in a short like 90 minute window, which is insane. Now, obviously, they didn't commit murder, they pushed somebody off of a cliff and they all think that they committed murder though. And so, people offers are bawling their eyes out and then like, no, you didn't actually kill them. But they set up the social situation to be able to do that. And when I first heard that, I was like, oh my gosh, this is the kind of show I want to watch. So I started watching it and it's crazy because if you watch what they do, like the way he was able to do this, to get somebody to go from zero to murder in 90 minutes, imagine if you just saw that with somebody, like, can you get them to buy your product in 90 minutes? Yes, you can, right? So that was kind of the premise of it. But it starts at the beginning with like asking people like simple things and getting people conforming to requests, to ideas, things like that. And they start really small. I think it's bigger and bigger and bigger until eventually asking for them to commit murder, right? Or to push someone for me, it's to get the sale. Said, is how to instill, spend your competitors and ethically steal all potential customers in your market. When I first wrote this headline, someone said, that's kind of aggressive, don't you think? I said, not for the 10X crowd, they're going to love this. All right. Now, May 6, 1965, something amazing happened. For decades and decades prior to that, human beings believed that it was physically impossible to break the four-minute mile. But on that day, May 6, 1965, Roger Bannister did it. Now, what's interesting is, again, decades, centuries prior to that, nobody believed it was possible. But as soon as Roger Bannister did it, people thought it was possible. In fact, just 46 days later, John Landy, because he saw what Roger Bannister did, he thought, if he can do it, I can do it. And just 46 days, he did it. And he broke the four-minute mile as well. And since then, dozens and dozens of people have done it. But it wasn't until one person did it that everyone else believed it was possible. August 17, 2004 was the day the four-minute mile was broken for me. At the time, I was a young kid. I just learned about this whole internet thing. I was geeking out. I was making about a thousand dollars a month. And I thought I was the smartest guy in the world. And on August 17, I was 24 years old at the time, this man, John Reese, he set out to do something that nobody thought was possible. He said, I'm going to make a million dollars in a day. And he went out there and he put together the internet campaign, put a really cool funnel together. He launched it. And in just 18 hours, they passed the million dollar mark. Now, me as a 24-year-old kid is sitting there looking at this with my goals and aspirations were to make a thousand dollars a month online. And I was like, if John Reese, if this guy who's just like me, just like any of us, can make a million dollars in a day, I wonder if I can make a million dollars in a year. First time it ever crossed my mind. I was like, I don't even know that's possible. But if John can do a million dollars in a day, I think I could do a million dollars in a calendar year. That was my goal. And so I started on January 1, 2005. I said, okay, this is the year. I'm going to make it in a calendar year. And I launched all these different things. And in 12 months, I missed the goal. I didn't hit a million dollars in sales. I got close, but I didn't hit it. It was 2005, 2006. I tried again. January 1st, I was getting a million dollars in the next 12 months. I launched a bunch of stuff again. And I didn't make it again. And then the next year, it was January 2007, I launched it. And by October 16th, I crossed the million dollar mark in a calendar year. Thank you. Because John did it, I knew that I could do it. Now, since then, I've gone and had multiple million dollars years, had a million dollar months, and even a million dollar days. But it wasn't until I knew that John could do it that I could do it. Now, for my community, in fact, how many funnel hackers do we have out here in the audience right now? A couple of things. I'll just make a piece of something I want to talk about. In fact, I have one of my friends who's a speaker. What he'll do is, he'll find like the happy faces in the audience, and he'll walk out, and like, he'll go towards someone, and he'll like, he'll touch somebody on the shoulder who he's talking to, who's like the happy face. And what happens is like, because there's like rapport here and here, it creates rapport around as well. It's kind of hard to explain or to understand, but it's true. It's like, when I'm getting all these people to pop up their hands, other people are seeing like, oh man, this person who's in proximity to me already has rapport with Russell, and it builds more rapport that fast as well. If that makes sense. It's kind of a weird ninja thing, but that's one big reason. I knew we had a lot of our people out in the audience, and I'm doing that so that everyone else is like, someone that, you know, I'm up on stage, so it might be hard to get rapport there, but it's like, hey, there's this person right here. In fact, Darren Stevens, one of my friends, he, he does this, his live events where he will work and get a rapport with like one person, and then he'll, you know, a couple people, and then he'll break into groups where those people are there, and the people get rapport together as a group, and then come back from, from group work back to main work, and also there's like rapport happening everywhere. So it's such a big thing, like rapport leads to believability, it leads to all these other things. They both graduated from the same school at the same time and they didn't see each other for 20 years. 20 years later, they met at a high school reunion and they found the one person had a, you know, worked in the nice corporation, had a good high paying job, he's really happy. And the other person was a multi, multi-millionaire. And looking from the outside, it looked like they were on the same trajectory, same path. But you see the power behind it. Basically I was showing like, look, I'm going to show you, I'm going to convince you this is the only way to 10 X your business is this process. So what's the big result they want? The only way to get is that. Ken, the expert seekers book, I have a, we talk about this a little bit and or basically I just slide it very big in like, if I can get you to believe this and you have to do this and everyone always gets so, it's funny how people get so stuck up on that, that piece of it, but it's very simple. It's like, what's the result you're promising them? What is the new opportunity you're offering them? And it's basically, if I can convince you, this new opportunity is going to get your result, you're going to go all in and then say, okay, this is what's going to cost and then make that very simple. Like for me, 10 X in your business goes from, you know, if you're 100 grand and making millions, so give me 11 grand for a million. Yes. Your million is 10, 10 is 100. Like in the analysis, it's like, okay, 11 grand is actually really, really cheap. If you actually can 10 X my business. Okay. So they've do the price marinade. Now I just got to come back and prove to them. We got to shout out that funnels are they key to 10 X in your business. And if so, then they have to give me 11,000 bucks because if they believe it, then they believe that's a million dollars that I'm trading for 11,000. Now it's like a no brainer. Right. So that's kind of the goal of the price marinade and how the whole thing all worked. Okay. I want to understand you guys, everyone of you guys in this room, you were literally one funnel away from 10 X in your company. Okay. The life you want, the marriage you want, the family you want is going to be fueled by the businesses we build. Okay. The funnels are the key. So those of you know me, my name is Russell Brunson. I am a serial entrepreneur with an obsession of helping entrepreneurs around the world. Nothing gets me more excited than that. I've had a chance to work with some of the most amazing people in the world. I built funnels for people like Tony Robbins, like Marcus, the owners from the profit. How many of you guys actually watched the TV show, The Prophet? Yeah. So last year they actually had me on the show and I had a chance to build funnels for one of Marcus's companies and it was insanely cool. So this is something that is kind of on the new side, but man, it's starting to get more and more mainstream. The more that I talk about this, okay. My entire goal is to get everybody to understand that sales funnels are the key to growing all of our companies. Okay. So what is a sales funnel? For those who don't know, let me start actually with what you probably do know. Most of us know what a traditional website is, right? Okay. I always call this the anti-sales department. We send people to traditional websites and what it's like is kind of like having a salesperson there and when somebody comes, they hand them a brochure and then they step back and they hope and they pray that the sale gets made. Now how many of you guys know if you had a salesperson who was handing out brochures and then hiding, you would fire them instantly? Yeah. If for some reason we're okay with our websites doing nothing, right? Okay. For all of us who are entrepreneurs, who are spending our own money out of our own pockets to grow our companies, we have to be smarter, which to build something where we put a dollar in advertising in and instantly get $2 back out. Okay. And that is what a sales funnel is. A sales funnel is kind of like taking your very best salesperson that you have, cloning that person and making it so that every single person that comes to your website, they grab them, they introduce themselves, they find out what they're interested in, taking that to buy the actual product and service that they need to help grow and have more success. Okay. That is what a sales funnel is. Much better way to do it. Okay. Now after 10 years of doing this business, you guys, I've tried a whole bunch of crazy stuff. I'm going to break it all down. Very simple, very easy. And so you guys, because you're here today and you woke up this morning, you get to cheat and get it the easy way. How many of you guys want it the easy way? Yeah. All right. So let me show you the hard way because funnels used to be hard. It's why nobody ever did them for the first decade of my game because that was in this game because they were hard to do. Okay. This is a sales funnel. I'm going to show you guys behind the scenes of this one. This is one of my supplement companies that we sold last year. Okay. Now to build this funnel, it doesn't look that complicated. I took pictures and put them on my wall so I wanted to see what it all looked like. It's only what, one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight pages. Yeah. That's probably pretty easy. Probably takes an hour or two, right? No. This is what we had to go through to create this. So step number one to create a funnel is we had to design the funnel, like the strategy. What's it going to be like? What's the upsells and the downsells and the price points to figure that out? Right? Number two, I had to write the copy. The copy is the sales letter. Now our supplement was helping people diabetic neuropathy. I didn't know what that meant. In fact, I made a rule inside of my company. I didn't want anyone to explain to me what diabetic neuropathy was. So we made at least a million dollars in the funnel. So I had no idea what we were even selling other than we knew it worked. And so I hired a copywriter to write the sales letter. It cost $20,000 for the sales letter. Then we had our designer to design the sales letter and a website designer to put it online, a programmer to hook the shopping carts and the auto responders to get it all working, analytics person. My upfront cost to build this one funnel alone was $29,000. Okay. Now that was not too bad. The worst thing though is that I had already ordered the supplements. They were sitting in a warehouse gathering dust for eight weeks. That's all I took is to build it two months for me to build this funnel. I had a chance to find launch it and it was awesome. But that's why most people have struggled in this business for so long. And when you understand this, everything else becomes really, really easy. Whoever can spend the most money to acquire customer wins. Number two, I want to show you guys how to get the best salesperson on planet earth to close sales for your product 24 hours a day, seven days a week. And the best thing about the salesperson is they never complain. They never ask for a raise. They never take a break. Now I am a salesperson. It's like most of you in this room. And what I've noticed about most of us salespeople, we're kind of high maintenance. We always want to get paid more. We want better leads. We want more stuff. Right? Okay. The salesperson about to introduce you guys, who doesn't do any of that stuff and it works 24 hours a day, seven days a week. And the last thing I'm going to show you is how to get all of the customers who are currently going to your competitors start coming to you instead. And it's going to be really fun. So let's dive right in. So second one, whoever can spend the most money to acquire a customer wins. Now this is the key to understand, you guys. I learned this from my first mentor, is Dan Kennedy. He said, business beats his competition by making the exact same prospect worth more to his business than to that of his competitors. Whoever can spend the most money to acquire customer wins. I'm going to jump into this for you guys to understand. So I launched my company ClickFunnels about three years ago. When we did it, it was me and a couple of dudes in an office that we were a little tiny office. And all we had was a vision of how to change the world. That was it. Okay. We had no money. We had no venture back cap. We had nothing like that. We just had a dream and a vision. Okay. Now as we were developing this company, this is us literally whiteboarding ClickFunnels on the wall and these guys coding it while I was just getting excited because I don't know how to code. I'm just excited all the time. And they were working on it. At the time, we're like, well, who's our competitors? Who are we competing against? And I found these are our two competitors. The first competitor got $38 million in funding and the second one got $127 million in funding. And I'm like, all right, well, that's what I'm going up against. I literally have a visa with a thousand dollar limit on it and a dream and a vision. How am I going to win? Okay. All these guys can completely outspend me. Yet we were able to take ClickFunnels from zero to over $100 million in sales in just three years, demolishing both of those two competitors without taking on a penny of outside capital. Thank you. Thank you. Now, since we started doing this, we have a whole bunch of venture capitalists who keep coming to like, Russell, can we give you money? I'm like, no, why would I take your money? I want my customers to give me money. That's the way business should happen. And they keep trying to give us money. And finally, one day I was just curious. I'm like, what did they kept talking about valuations? They're like, well, I want to see what they think we're actually worth. I had no idea. Like, this is a whole world that's beyond me. I just want to sell stuff. And so we went to lunch with one of these VCs and they started asking all these questions. And he said, okay, how much does it cost to acquire a customer? That's like, that's question number one, right? If you guys watch Shark Tank, they always ask like, what's the cost to acquire a customer? And I was like, well, when we drive ads, we buy a bunch of ads and we drive them to clickfunnels.com, it costs us about $120 to get a free trial. And the guy was like, like licking his chops. He's like, okay, so if we gave you $50 million in funding that equals X amount of customers and do all the math in his head. And I was like, whoa, slow down, slow down. I was like, we actually turned those ads off. He's like, what? Why would you turn those ads off? Like you're acquiring customers, $120 a person, that's awesome. I was like, because I have to pay for those, like I have to pull my wallet out and pay for everyone those customers. Like I can't float $120 for every free customer who signs up. And he's like, so what do you do? And I said, well, I didn't want to be broke. So we had to do things different. We had to think smarter about things. So I said, this is how we do it. We run ads and I don't sell clickfunnels up front. Instead, what I do is I sell, I drive people to a sales funnel. And I've got a whole bunch of sales funds, about five or six sales funnels we use right now. We drive people through and we sell them for things. How many of you guys have purchased one of my books? All right. You guys have been through the funnel then. Okay. On average, right now, it costs about $20 in ads to sell one copy of my book. Now you guys know I give away my book for free plus shipping. But the cool thing is that when someone comes and buys, they go through this funnel. So that means there's an upsell. There's a downsell, the audio book, training program, a bunch of things like that. So right now on average, I spend $20 on ads to sell a book and I make about $40. So what just happens is that I get $20 cash net profit into my pocket and I get a customer and then I tell the customer, Hey, there's really cool software called ClickFunnels. You should try it. And they try it for free. And it doesn't not cost me $120 to get a customer. I got paid $20 before I even told that customer about what I'm trying to sell them. He was getting this so the VC didn't. I explained it once. I spent a second time. He's like, wait, wait, I don't understand. So people pay you to become a lead and then they read your book and they're completely indoctrinating your belief system and then they sign up and it doesn't cost you anything. I'm like, yeah, it's really awesome. And he's like three or four times and finally, I remember the very last time he said, if what you're saying is true, this will change business as we know it forever. I said, yeah, that's my mission. That's what I'm trying to tell people. You understand this one funnel was worth more to me than $127 million in funding. We blew past both of these companies in less than three years. That's the power sales funnels. All right. All right. Really quick. Want to jump in and just if you notice what I just did, because it's pretty ninja cool, is now in people's mind, they believe that a funnel is worth $127 million. Right? I'm always trying to price anchor things together. Like because if people think your stuff's worth, $20, that's going to spend the things worth a million bucks, they're going to spend more. Right? So I just proved that like you could get $127 million in VC funding or you could just get a quick funnel's account and it'll do it better and smarter and more profitably. Right? So we're trying to like price compare like apples, orange comparisons. Right? It's not an apples to apples comparisons. We can now spend everybody. Okay, that's the key. So question for all of you guys, lots of different business types. And there's 9,000 people, you all have different business. The question I'm going to get from most you guys, what kind of funnel should I use? It depends on the kind of business you have. So I kind of went through and we segmented before I came here to figure out all the different markets you guys are in. We broke down about 10. Okay. First one are people that either run an agency or they're freelancer. How many of you guys in that fit? You're who you are. There we go. Number two, people selling information products. Number three, see people selling e-commerce products. Number four, people doing coaching or consulting. Number five, people that are network marketing. Number six, local businesses doing anything in chiropractic, dental. A lot of business to business people just looking for leads to generate. We've got a whole bunch of people who are bloggers or affiliate marketers. We've got nonprofits. And then we've got a bunch of people who are just getting started. Is that kind of some of all you guys? Is that cover everybody? Okay. The reason why I'm doing this is in a perfect world, again, I'm doing one-on-one selling, right? I can be like, what's your business? They tell me, cool, let me show you how funnels work for you. But I'm in a room at 9,000. It's mass selling. I can't do that. And in a perfect world, I'd create a special presentation for every business type and show to them, but I don't have that opportunity. So I hook them to the idea of a funnel, but now I got to prove to them that a funnel is actually for their specific business. One of the biggest problems I had at the first 10x event that I solved in the second one is after the first 10x event, I did the clothes, we sold a bunch of people in the hallway, I got asked probably 100 times, like, hey, will funnels work for like B2B? And I was like, yes. I got literally in the old presentation, it's like, it works for this and this and this and this. But I didn't say these three magic letters, B2B. And because of that, I probably lost 100 times 2000. So $200,000 in sales plus, because I didn't say they were B2B. Because they're segments like, ah, it's not for me. So I'm trying to show them, like, this works for every business in here. I'm going to make it all inclusive. So I show the 10 submarkets, the premise touch almost everybody I can dream of. And I'm going to go even deeper with that. Because I need them to all believe, like, yes, this funnel thing is for me. Okay. Again, that's the difference between selling one on one that you have to do different amasses. Like, I have to group all the different people in and bring them in and get them to believe it all at once. Okay. So that's kind of how I did. I brought them all in once. And you'll see, I'm going to do some more stuff to make them all believe that no matter what business type they're in, that it's going to work for them. Hopefully there's no one I missed, right? So I want you to understand that you guys remember the commercial from back in the 90s when the iPhone first came out? And they're like, there's a nap for that. Oh, you need this? There's a nap for that. Okay. Just like there's an app for that, I promise you there's a funnel for that. Whatever business you're in, there's a funnel for that. Okay. So the question then is, how do you know what type of funnel to build? Okay. And we do that. We figure out what that is through a very fun, simple process that we call funnel hacking. That's where you guys all get your new nickname. Okay. So let me explain how funnel hacking works. I learned the first part is I learned from Tony Robbins. Tony Robbins told me, said, if you want to achieve success, all you need to do is find a way to model those who've already succeeded, right? Now I started thinking back in my career of things I've been good at. And I was a wrestler growing up. How many wrestlers do we have in the room? Yeah. Anyone on a wrestle? I would much rather do that right now up here. All right. Let's go. Okay. So I was a wrestler and when I first got started, I was not only not good, I was bad. My first year, I got beat almost every single time. And remember the end of the season, my dad took me to the state tournament and state tournament. One of the guys on my high school team won the state title. It was a three time state champ. My dad videotaped what he did and we watched it. And that whole summer, we would watch the video of this guy and my dad would teach me the moves that he was doing. So all summer long, I practiced and practiced and I practiced. I found someone who's good. I modeled them. And in a very short period of time, I went from being a really bad wrestler to being a state champ to going to high school national, taking second place in the country and becoming an All-American, eventually going to college, being one of the top 10 wrestlers in the country. Okay. Not because, thank you. Not because I was an amazing wrestler, but because I found people who are super successful and modeled what worked. When I started business, it was the same thing. I went to college. I went to college to wrestle. People asked my degree and I was like, my degree is in wrestling. I didn't even know the classes were there. I barely graduated. And towards the end of it, I started learning about this internet thing. I didn't take business classes. I didn't take entrepreneur classes. I didn't take any. I just knew that I wanted to make some money. I jumped online. I started seeing people who were successful. I'm like, man, these people are geniuses. I don't know how to do what they do, but I can see what they're doing. I'm just going to model them. So I started modeling what they were doing. And within a year of graduating from college, I made over a million dollars online. Okay. The same thing is true in sales funnels. You guys don't have to be sales funnel geniuses. You don't have to geek out like I do every single day. I get his look around and find someone in your industry or in a similar industry who's having success and then we model them. Okay. Every time I share this, I get somebody who's like, well, that's cool. Russell, but I got a better way to do it. I got a different thing I can do instead. And I always smile at those people. I say, you can always tell who the pioneers are because they've got arrows in their back and they're lying down face down in the dirt. Okay. In this industry, you don't want to be a pioneer. You don't want to go try things. First time I ever got this, I was actually at an event and there was a guy on stage. His name is Porter Stansbury. And Porter worked for a company called the Gora Publishing. How many of you guys know about the Gora? They do over $1.5 billion a year selling financial newsletters, biggest financial newsletters in the world. Okay. And he says, and Porter's up there on stage. He ran a division called Stansbury Research, which was the biggest division at the time. And he's going through all his numbers and his metrics. He's showing his sales funnels. I'm like a little kid in the back like, I can't believe he's telling me this stuff. I was freaking out, right? Taking notes and everything. He's talking a million miles a minute. And all of a sudden he stopped. He said, you know what's interesting? He said, every single year there's people who see what we're doing here at the Gora Publishing and they want to compete against us. So he said, the first thing they come out and they try to beat us on pricing. So they come in and they sell their product for cheaper. He's like, we know from decades of doing this that you cannot make those numbers work. The metrics just don't work. He says, so then they figure that out eventually. They try something different. They try three or four things eventually they go bankrupt and they lose their business. Okay. The biggest thing that kills all entrepreneurs is that not that they don't have vision. They do have vision, but usually they either run out of time or money before they have the ability to execute on the vision. Okay. And Porter said at this time, said, if you want to compete with the Gora Publishing, you can't try to compete with us. He said, you got to copy us. And that's when light bulb went off my head. I thought, man, it Gora's got hundreds of full-time copywriters writing copy to split testing things. If I want to go and try all those things, I don't have the money. I don't have the budget to do that, but I can go and look at like all of the pioneers that had all the arrows in the back and move here like, where are they today? Oh, they're right here. Oh, that's what's working today. I should just model that. And that's what we do. Okay. That's the way of success so quickly is by finding people who have already succeeded and we model them. Okay. All right. So step number one in this funnel hacking process is find somebody to funnel hack. So when we were watching our supplement company, it was my first supplement company, and we were selling to people who had diabetic neuropathy. And I was doing some research like, who else out there is having success? There's got to be somebody. And we found this little website right here. And we're going to and looking at, I was like, this is probably the ugliest website I think I've ever seen in my life. It's all purple and black and weird. But I did some research and some intel and I found out about at the time that they were making about $100,000 a month selling this supplement. I was like, okay, cool. They know what they're doing, but I'm a marketing guy. So I'm going to do it better because I know a better way to do it. And then I was like, wait a minute, maybe they have some arrows in their back. Like, I don't know what people with diabetic neuropathy like. Maybe they know something I don't know. So instead of trying to be creative and do my own thing, I said, look, I'm just going to model what works. So I went out with my very first version of our sales page. It looked very similar. Like, and one thing I want to stress is that notice that I said model, we didn't copy. Okay. I modeled the look, the field, the layout, the price points. Like they had a big logo on left hand side. I had a logo. They had a phone number on right hand side. I had a phone number. They had sales letter, the picture of the bottle. I modeled the look, the field, the layout. It's close to like good. I didn't copy anything, but I modeled the structure. And we launched this in a very short period of time. This little website started making us about $20,000 a month. Okay. Which is cool. And then I was like, oh, there's got to be something else they're doing that I'm missing. Like, what is it? How are they having some of success? And so I did what every good entrepreneur has to become good at doing. Pulled my wallet out of my pocket, pulled my credit card out and I bought their product. Like what I do behind the scenes that I'm missing. Now, unfortunately for me and also for them, they didn't have much on the back end. They just kind of ended. I was like, God, there's got to be somebody out here knows what they're doing in this industry. So I went outside and I started looking for other companies. And eventually I found a supplement company and I met the owner of, and they were doing about $30 million a month selling their supplement. I'm like, okay, this guy knows what he's doing. And so if you look at this, this, this exercise is basically number one, like what are all the faults beliefs somebody has about your vehicle? And the same thing with internal, external, right. But what's all the faults beliefs that somebody has? Okay. So write down those faults beliefs here. And then number two is like, okay, what do they, what do they experience that made them believe that thing? Okay. And number three is what's the story are they telling themselves that, that, that, that's keeping them? Then what's the story you can tell is going to trump their story? Okay. So I'm going to illustrate this. I always share this with the network marketing thing. It's the easiest one to kind of explain. So I'll use it. So let's just say here's a, if the, if the, if the vehicle I'm trying to get somebody in is, is a false belief. So let's say here's step number one. Okay. This is their fault. So what are all the faults beliefs somebody has? So I'm going to list them out like number one, number two, number three and list out as many faults, please, as I can think of. Okay. So let's just say one false belief is like, if I joined a network marketing company, I'm going to lose my friends and my family. Okay. So that's a false belief. I'm going to lose my friends and family. Okay. So I'll list that down. Number two then is, okay, what is the experience that they had? They're remembering, right? They're remembering some kind of experience where they got beat up. That's why the guy's a black guy. Okay. They had a bad experience. What is the experience that they had that made them believe this false belief? Okay. So usually in network marketing, the experience is one of two things. Number one is they joined a program one day. They called their friends and their family and their mom and their sister and their brother and every single person got annoyed and like stopped returning their calls. So they lost, actually lost friends and family because of it. Or the opposite side of that is they were on the receiving end of that and their sister got involved in something. She called them every single day and text message and coming over to the point where her sister, that they loved, she's been avoiding forever, right? So there's the experience. So sister annoyed me, right? Or whatever. So that's why that experience gave them the story of why they will never do it. Okay. So the third thing then is figuring out, okay, what's the story that they're telling themselves? Okay. If you study Tony Robbins, he always talks about this. Everyone's got a story. And so their problems are negative, but they've got this negative story. So the story, it's very similar to this. The story is like, if I tell my friends and my family about this thing, then, like if then statement, then I'm going to lose my friends and family, right? So that's the story. And then they have. So then your job here in this part is you got to look at what is the new epiphany bridge that you have that trumps their story. Okay. My story is if I do this, then this. So you come back and say, look, I used to think the same thing. In fact, when I got started, this right day, I called my friends and my family was annoying. I lost my sister and my friends. They won't talk to me anymore. And I was like, I'll never do this again. But then I met this person. And this person, they're having huge success and they try to get me. And I said, no, I'm never going to do that again. But that person told me that like, they don't talk to friends and family either. They actually use the internet and they use the internet to generate leads. And now they get people signing up and they build the business so big. Now their friends and family ask them, like, what are you doing? But they never talk to anybody unless they ask them first because of the internet. Okay. So I tell this story, like the internet is how I get leads. And all of a sudden I tell them this story and then they look at them like, oh, wow, my story isn't true. My story sucks. If I do it your way, like this is actually the truth. I could actually do that things I wanted the result. Right. And now this is the story I tell them that story and trumps their story, which then changes the experience, then changes the false belief and that false belief disappears. And now it was replaced with their new belief. Okay. So for me, if I'm doing secret number one on the vehicle, I'm sitting down and say, okay, here's the vehicle. What are all the false beliefs somebody's going to have about funnels? What's all the false beliefs about ketosis? What's all the false beliefs they're going to have about whatever product or service you sell and list those things out just like if I was doing a one-on-one sales call with somebody, what else is your false belief? Why won't you buy? Why won't you buy? Why won't you buy? And then listen to them tell me all their different stories about why they're not going to buy. Then I'm going to sit back and say, okay, what did they probably experience that made them believe that? Would they experience make them believe that? Would they make experience make them believe that? And then what's the story they're telling themselves? So I'm translating this experience into a story. And the reason why I do this is because I'm going to be making a story that trumps it. A lot of people are like, this is like almost the same thing. Yes. This is this is the experience they had, but this is the story that they're telling themselves in their head. So experience and story, experience and story. And I got to figure out what's the story I'm going to have to tell over here to trump that. So for me, when I'm doing secret number one, I'm teaching people about funnels and things. What are all the false beliefs? Faults beliefs is that it's not going to work my industry. Why do they believe that? Because they try their stuff and because they're a photographer, they don't think it's going to work for them. What's the story that doesn't work for photographers? I need to show a story that's going to break this belief that they're like, oh my gosh, I'm a photographer. Oh my gosh, I'm an e-com. Oh, I'm a network marketing. So I destroy all the people whose false belief is not going to work for their industry. Then it's like, well, funnels are really hard and complicated. Okay. Why do they believe that? Well, they tried to hire an ad agency in the past. It was confusing. So what happened when they went and they built this confusing thing and it didn't work and it always sings and cost them a ton of money. It was frustrating. Okay. Well, what's my story? My story is different. All I did is I found some of these are successful. I bought their product. I looked at what it looked like and they modeled it. So I tell that story. I'll sense like, oh my gosh, I don't have to hire an agency. It's actually easy. And I go to the next one. The next one. So like all I'm doing in secret number one is looking at here's all the false beliefs that they have about the vehicle I'm trying to get them in. What's the experience they have because of that? Okay, most of my videos today are all done on my phone. I pop out of the popsocket, I can hold like this. Okay, this is how it works. And I turn on the camera. And now I can sell everything by answering those four questions. Number one, who are you? Number two, what do you have for your person who's watching this video? Number three, why do they need it? I'm not blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, blah, right? So I'm like, I got to show them how simple this. Okay. So I showed them the skills that I know they all have. The selling part is done. Now let me show you the pages. It's super easy and simple. Let me show how simple it is to make them believe like, oh, cool. I could do that. That's all I want them to have is like, oh, I actually could do that. Even I could do that. Right. That was my whole goal showing that with the secret secret to showing how simple and how easy it is so that if they can make a video on the phone, they can be able to use this. Okay. And so a couple of things I want to stress here because some of you guys have products that are demoable like software. Some of you guys don't either way it's fine. Even if you don't have software, you can demo, you can still do the same concept by kind of explaining, here's how the process works and showing the whole thing. One thing interesting I found is, and this is going to be tougher. Those of you guys who were like obsessed with your product, right? The creators and the trainers, that kind of stuff. You don't want to teach or train or like show some stuff. And so I remember when ClickFunnels, we added a whole bunch of new features. I went back and I added my demo video. I added all these things how you can do like all the ninja cool features. I'm like, oh, people are going to love this stuff. And I added all those things in and my demo video is about twice as long. And I showed like everything that was possible inside of ClickFunnels. And when I did that, my sales dropped to almost nothing. It was like the worst thing I possibly could have ever done. And so I went back to simplification like, let me show you the big idea is like it's easy. You can drag, you can drag, move things around, and kind of show them really quickly. And then it was done. Every time there's the next ClickFunnels killer that comes out. In fact, now I think we're 38, the big one launching right now. And I go and I watch their demo video and they're showing them demoing 8,000 features. I was like, oh, good. I'm good for another round of ClickFunnels killers. Okay. They don't want to know everything that's happening. Again, it comes back to seeming like the reason why in the presentation, I'm not going to show you all of the how to do everything is because that's not what they need right now. Okay, if I show them everything about software, it's like they're not they're going to be so overwhelmed, they're not going to do it. Okay, and now how many guys make most of your primary decisions because you're trying to move away from pain? When I think about that, and one thing that's interesting is if you look at it, traditionally most entrepreneurs make choices because they're trying to move towards pleasure, but the majority of the world makes decisions trying to move, trying to stay away from pain, right? And so what happens a lot of times is lots of entrepreneurs get on stage and will speak, or try to sell, or try to sell people like towards pleasure, towards pleasure, you're going to have a house and a mansion, it's going to be amazing, you're going to have six pack abs, all that kind of stuff. Whereas the majority of people, that's not what they're looking for, they're looking to get out of pain, okay? So like, I just hate my boss, I don't want to go to work anymore, nice pants are tight, I don't feel comfortable in my own skin, like those are the things and so if you notice that I do this presentation, I'm hitting all angles, I'm hitting from like moving towards pleasure, all the way down to away from pain, because I'm trying to, I'm trying to contact each of those different personality types in the audience and make sure all of them understand like how this fits for what their actual needs are, okay? So a couple things. Energy when you're on stage is so important. If you talk normally like this and you're on stage and you're talking your normal thing you would want face to face with the person, you're going to sound like you are sound asleep. Okay? You have to have your energy like 10 times higher to sound normal. So you guys notice my energy is like, oh, I'm going crazy the whole time. Like that feels normal, especially in a room that big, you have to have way high energy. I've been talking about this and I've been doing it and a lot of people don't catch it. Dana Derrick, she's one of our inner circle, my inner circle members, he, he told me one thing. It's like one of the biggest things I learned from you is like, he's like, I have to amplify my energy to get people to ascend to me, right? To come to you. And for you who are going to be a presenter or stage, like you have to have way more energy than normal. Okay? Number two, it's okay to talk fast. In fact, it actually helps you. You will notice I talk fast normally. That's not a down thing. I want, if I'm talking fast people will hear stuff and if they don't get everything that's okay because it's like, I don't know, I feel the energy of this. I'm excited. I don't know exactly what it is, but like, I know I need that. So talking fast isn't bad. And you'll notice as I transition from the teaching to the close, I actually increase the speed of my speaking. That is intentional because that's just the pacing of how it works. Okay? He's slowly pacing down during the close. People fall asleep. They'll get tired of the walk out and those kinds of things. So during the close, I'm actually picking up my pace, not lowering it down. Okay? So while I'm apologizing for speaking fast, like I'm speaking fast on purpose. Okay? Same thing. I was at Tony Robbins' event. It's, if you run Tony's event, it's freezing cold. He makes the room be like, we hire Tony. It's like, the room has to be like 20 degrees or something crazy. I don't know. He gets on stage. He's always like, it's freezing up here. Can you just turn the heat on? Even though he knows, everyone knows in the back, you don't touch the, you don't touch the heat. Tony wants it cold. This way, it's like people are like, oh, he is rapport, right? So I apologize for speaking fast, but I don't mind. That's strategic. I'm speaking fast on purpose. Okay? And so don't feel bad about that. But it gets anyway. So there you go. All right. So I want to stress that out because I know some people like Russell, you talk too fast.
